title: Chapter 22: Muscle Memory
content: 3 AM, and Leo is talking to his variables.

"Chloe, I need you to—no, that's not right." He blinks at the screen, then squints at the function he's been writing. He's been writing the same function for six hours. I know because I've been watching him rewrite it from the kitchen doorway, the same twenty lines appearing and disappearing, a digital tide that never reaches shore.
"Chloe." He says my name like a variable now. Casual. Assignable. He doesn't even look up.
"Leo."
"I need the bot signature array to—Chloe, wait, which Chloe am I—" He stops. Rubs his eyes. The light from his laptop makes him look like a ghost renting space in our apartment. "Which one are you?"
"The real one. Close the laptop."
"I can't. The botnet's adapting every fifteen minutes. If I stop, we lose the—"
"Close the laptop."

He finally looks at me. His eyes are pink-rimmed, sandpaper-dry. There's a red mark on his cheek from resting it against his wrist while he typed. He smells like soap and circuit boards—that particular Leo cocktail of drugstore body wash and overheated silicon. Twenty hours. He's been at this for twenty hours.
"Five more minutes," he says.
"You said that three hours ago."
"Did I?" He checks the time. His face does something complicated—confusion, then alarm, then a kind of bone-deep tiredness that makes his shoulders drop two inches. "Oh."

I walk over. He watches me like I might be another hallucination—something his sleep-deprived brain cooked up to convince him to stop. I close his laptop. Gently but firmly, the way you'd close a book on a story someone's been reading too long.
"Hey—"
"Leo. You're mistaking me for a data structure. You need to sleep."
"I need to stop them from—"

"You need to not collapse and hit your head on the desk and make everything worse." I go to the kitchen. Fill a glass of water. Come back and put it in his hand. He looks at it like he's forgotten what glass is. Then he drinks. Half of it in one go, water spilling down his chin.
I take the glass from him and sip from the other side. Tepid. New York tap. The faintest taste of the lemon we keep in the fridge but never use. I set it down.
"Couch. Now."
"I can't just—"
"Couch."

He stands up. Wobbles. Catches himself on the desk edge. I steer him toward the couch by the shoulder, and he lets himself be steered, which is how I know he's truly gone—he normally fights being managed like a cat fights a bath. He lies down. His head hits the cushion and he's out. Just like that. One second a person, the next a closed sign.

I stand over him. I don't feel tenderness. I don't feel warmth. I catalog this the way I catalog everything now: factually, from a distance, like reading my own vital signs off a monitor. Boyfriend asleep on couch. Should feel something. Don't.
But my hand reaches out.

It moves without permission—my fingers brushing the dark hair off his forehead, the way I must have done a hundred times before. His skin is warm. Clammy with exhaustion sweat. The hair falls right back where it was because it's too short to stay pushed aside.
I pull my hand back. Look at it. My fingers are warm now from his forehead and they don't know what to do with that information.
Muscle memory.
My body remembers. My mind doesn't.

I sit on the floor next to the couch. My back against the cushions, my knees pulled up. From here I can hear him breathing—slow and slightly congested, like he's been breathing recycled apartment air for too long. The apartment smells like him. Like us. Or what "us" used to smell like. Soap and circuit boards and the fabric softener I stopped noticing months ago.

I turn to study his face. The face I apparently looked at every day for over a year and felt something about. The exhaustion has carved him into something cruder—all planes and shadows, the softness of him worn away. But there's still the stubborn set of his jaw, even in sleep. The loyalty etched into the furrow between his brows, like his face got stuck in perpetual concern and never found its way back.
I try to feel what I should feel.
Gratitude: the recognition that someone has given you something valuable without obligation. I feel the recognition. I don't feel the warmth it's supposed to come wrapped in.

Love: a deep attachment characterized by prioritization of another's wellbeing alongside or above your own. I know the definition. I've looked it up. Four times. The word sits in my mind like a vocabulary flashcard—accurate but unpopulated, a house with no furniture.

Desire: a wanting. I can want things. I want the botnet to stop. I want the 50M milestone to freeze. I want my emotions back. But wanting Leo? That's a different species of wanting, and I can't find the habitat.

Safety: the absence of threat combined with the presence of trust. I can acknowledge that I'm safe with him. I just can't experience the sensation of safety—the exhale, the unclenching, the full-body permission to stop performing. That's locked in a room I don't have the key for.
I can define every one. I can't experience any of them.
But my hand is still resting on the cushion next to his head. It went there without permission and it's staying without permission, like a dog that found its way home and refuses to leave the porch.
I look at my hand. My traitor hand.

His phone is on the floor next to the couch. Screen down, the way he always leaves it. He never locks it around me. Not because he's careless—Leo is many things but careless isn't one of them. He doesn't lock it because he decided, at some point, that I should have access to everything. That there should be no locked doors between us. That's not trust as a feeling. That's trust as a practice. Somewhere between the two, I think there's a difference that matters and I can't articulate what it is.
I pick up the phone. The screen wakes. Blue glow filling the dark apartment like bioluminescence. I open his photos.

Most of it is screenshots—code snippets, error logs, Discord conversations with other developers. But there's a cluster. A folder he's named "Important" with the kind of lowercase irony that's so Leo. I open it.
Me.

Me and Leo at Coney Island. I hit play on the video. The Cyclone. I'm screaming—mouth open, hair whipping, one hand white-knuckled on the lap bar and the other gripping his arm. He's laughing. Not at me—with me. The kind of laugh that's all teeth and squinted eyes and no composure whatsoever. The camera shakes because he's laughing too hard to hold it steady.

I watch myself scream. The woman on the screen feels terror and exhilaration and the specific giddy joy of surviving something terrifying with someone you trust. I can see all of that in her body language. I can read it like a textbook.
I just can't remember what it tasted like from the inside.

Next video. Her—me—cooking. I'm standing at the stove with a look of determined concentration, like I'm defusing a bomb. Smoke is rising. Leo's voice from behind the camera: "Babe, I think you should—" And then a small flare of fire, and me yelping, and Leo appearing in frame with a dish towel, smothering the pan fire with the practiced calm of someone who's done this before. The way he looks at me after the fire's out. Not annoyed. Not mocking. Just—fond. Helplessly, stupidly fond of the person who just set their dinner on fire.
I watch that look on his face and I think: I used to be the reason for that. I used to make that happen. Now I can't even feel it when it's directed at me.

Next. Rooftop. Midnight. The city sprawled out below us like a circuit board—his metaphor, probably, or mine, or ours. We're slow dancing. No music. Just his hand on the small of my back and my head on his shoulder and the distant sound of a siren weaving through traffic. We're barely moving. Just swaying. Two people who've found the one place in New York where stillness is possible.

I watch myself dance with him. The body language is so familiar—the way I lean into him, the way my fingers curl into the fabric of his shirt, the way I fit against him like I was designed to. But the internal state is locked. The video plays. I watch. It's like watching an actress play me. She's good. Convincing. She sure looks like someone in love.

I scroll past more. Birthdays. Lazy mornings. A video of me reading, unaware I'm being filmed—the kind of soft surveillance that should feel creepy but apparently didn't, apparently felt like being witnessed, like being worth watching.
Then one video stops me.

It's Leo. Alone. Selfie mode. The camera is close to his face—too close, the framing wrong, like he didn't mean to record. He's sitting at his desk, but a different desk—his old setup, the one in his bedroom before we moved in together. His eyes are red. Not from coding this time. From something else.

"I don't know if she's ever coming back." His voice is quiet. Rough. He's not performing—he doesn't know the camera's running, or he forgot, or he thought it was a photo and hit the wrong button. "The her I mean." He swallows. "But I keep coding because... because if there's even a chance that she can feel this again—that she can feel ME again—I have to try."

He looks away from the camera. Looks at something off-screen. When he looks back, his jaw is set and his eyes are wet and he says: "She made me feel like I was worth something when I was nothing. I owe her the same."
He reaches for the phone. Realizes it's recording video, not taking a photo. His expression shifts—embarrassed, caught. He fumbles with the screen. Taps something. The video doesn't delete.
It saves instead.
I watch it twice.

The first time, I'm parsing it. Analyzing. He's describing loyalty as a debt, which is reductive, but it's also the most Leo way to frame it—he doesn't understand his own heart so he translates it into transactions because that's a language he knows. He loves me in code. In functions that call themselves recursively, forever, because he never wrote an exit condition.
The second time, I'm not parsing.
The second time, something flickers in my chest.

It's not an emotion. Not even close. It's more like a signal—a dead channel that suddenly catches a fragment of transmission. Static shaped like meaning. A ghost of a feeling, the outline of love without any of the substance filling it in. Like seeing a skyline through fog. You can't touch the buildings. You can't walk the streets or open the doors or feel the city pulse beneath your feet. But you know they're there. The fog doesn't erase the skyline. It just hides it.
My hand goes to my chest. Presses flat against my sternum, like I'm checking for a heartbeat that shouldn't be there.
The ghost pulse is there. Faint. Irregular. The phantom limb of love.
And it's terrifying.

Not because it feels bad—it doesn't feel bad, it doesn't feel good, it doesn't feel like anything I have vocabulary for. It's terrifying because if I can almost feel it, that means the deduction hasn't taken it completely. Which means there's something left. Some stubborn cellular memory, some emotional scar tissue that Mephisto's algorithm hasn't scraped away yet.
Which means there's something left to lose.
On the couch, Leo shifts. A small sound—not quite a word, not quite a breath. Then, clearer, thick with sleep: "Chloe?"
I don't answer. I put his phone down. Screen off. Blue glow dies, and the apartment is dark again, and the only light is the faint amber bleed from the streetlights through the curtains.

"You should sleep," he mumbles. His hand reaches out from the couch, finds my shoulder, squeezes once. His fingers are warm. Then they fall away and he's under again, pulled back under without a fight.
I sit in the dark. My shoulder is still warm where he touched it.

I get up. Go to my room—not our room, I've been sleeping in the guest room for weeks now, another fact I can catalog without feeling. I lie down on the bed. No sheets—just the mattress and a pillow and the grey light of a city that never goes fully dark.

The ghost pulse hasn't stopped. It's still there, faint and wrong, like a murmur a doctor would flag. The phantom limb of love. My body remembering something my mind can't access, reaching for a feeling that isn't there anymore, and finding just enough of an edge to know what's missing.
It doesn't feel good.

It feels like a wound that's about to be reopened wider. Like skin that's almost healed and someone's already reaching for the stitches. The almost-feeling is worse than nothing. Nothing is stable. Nothing is safe. Nothing can't be taken because there's nothing left. But almost—almost means there's something. Something fragile and fractional and barely alive, and Mephisto's algorithm is sixty thousand followers away from coming for it.
I stare at the ceiling. Water stain shaped like a continent nobody's discovered yet.
48.7 million.

The deduction is loading. I can feel it now—feel it in the ghost pulse, in the almost-presence, in the way my hand reached for Leo's forehead without permission and stayed without permission and wanted without knowing how to want. My body is keeping love alive like a patient on life support. The monitors are beeping. The signs are there. The algorithm just hasn't pulled the plug yet.
And now, for the first time since the empathy went, I have something left to lose that I can almost feel.
That's not comfort.
That's a target.
